full_context: The capacitor capacity at which resonance occurs can be calculated by: Q_{cx} = S_d \left( \frac{1}{n^2} - K \right). Where Q_{cx} is the capacitor capacity at which n-th harmonic resonance occurs (Mvar); S_d is the bus short-circuit capacity at the installation location of the shunt capacitor bank (MV·A); n is the harmonic order, i.e., the ratio of the harmonic frequency to the fundamental grid frequency; K is the reactor ratio.

variable_definitions.name: Resonant capacitor capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacitor capacity corresponding to n-th harmonic resonance

variable_definitions.name: Bus short-circuit capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Bus short-circuit capacity at the installation point of the shunt capacitor bank

variable_definitions.name: Harmonic order
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of harmonic frequency to the grid fundamental frequency

variable_definitions.name: Reactor ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of series reactor inductive reactance to capacitor capacitive reactance

potential_questions: Given S_d=1000MV·A, n=5, K=0.05, find Q_{cx}
task_description: 1. Obtain bus short-circuit capacity \(S_d\). 2. Determine harmonic order \(n\). 3. Determine reactor ratio \(K\). 4. Calculate \(1/n^2\). 5. Calculate \((1/n^2-K)\). 6. Compute \(Q_{cx}=S_d\times(1/n^2-K)\). 7. Output \(Q_{cx}\).

full_context: After connecting a series reactor, the operating voltage of the capacitor should be calculated as: U_c = \frac{U_s}{\sqrt{3}S} \cdot \frac{1}{1-K}. Where U_c is the capacitor operating voltage (kV); U_s is the bus operating voltage at the connection point of the shunt capacitor installation (kV); S is the number of series sections per phase of the capacitor bank; K is the reactor ratio.

variable_definitions.name: Capacitor operating voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Operating voltage actually applied to capacitor terminals

variable_definitions.name: Bus operating voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Bus voltage at the connection point of the shunt capacitor installation

variable_definitions.name: Number of series sections
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of capacitor sections connected in series per phase

variable_definitions.name: Reactor ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of series reactor inductive reactance to capacitor capacitive reactance

potential_questions: Given U_s=10kV, S=2, K=0.06, find U_c
task_description: 1. Obtain bus operating voltage \(U_s\). 2. Determine number of series sections \(S\). 3. Determine reactor ratio \(K\). 4. Compute \(\sqrt{3}\). 5. Compute \(U_s/(\sqrt{3}\times S)\). 6. Compute \((1-K)\). 7. Compute \(1/(1-K)\). 8. Compute \(U_c=[U_s/(\sqrt{3}\times S)]\times[1/(1-K)]\). 9. Output \(U_c\).

full_context: When a single capacitor bank of the same reactor ratio is switched on, or when additional banks are switched on, the inrush current should be calculated as: I_{\mathrm{*sm}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{K}} \left(1 - \beta \frac{Q_0}{Q}\right) + 1. \beta = 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{Q}{K S_d}}}. Q = Q' + Q_0. Where I_{\mathrm{*sm}} is the per-unit value of the inrush current peak; Q is the total capacitor-bank capacity installed on the same bus (Mvar); Q_0 is the capacity of the capacitor bank being switched on (Mvar); Q' is the total capacity of all capacitor banks currently in operation (Mvar); β is the source influence factor.

variable_definitions.name: Per-unit peak inrush current
variable_definitions.meaning: Per-unit inrush current peak referenced to the peak rated current of the switched capacitor bank

variable_definitions.name: Reactor ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of series reactor inductive reactance to capacitor capacitive reactance

variable_definitions.name: Source influence factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor reflecting the influence of source/system characteristics on inrush current

variable_definitions.name: Switched-in capacitor capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacity of the capacitor bank being switched on

variable_definitions.name: Total capacitor capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total capacity of all capacitor banks on the same bus

potential_questions: Given K=0.12, β=0.85, Q_0=8Mvar, Q=40Mvar, find I_{*sm}
task_description: 1. Determine reactor ratio \(K\). 2. Obtain source influence factor \(\beta\). 3. Obtain switched-in capacitor capacity \(Q_0\). 4. Obtain total capacitor capacity \(Q\). 5. Compute \(Q_0/Q\). 6. Compute \(\beta\times(Q_0/Q)\). 7. Compute \(1-\beta\times(Q_0/Q)\). 8. Compute \(1/\sqrt{K}\). 9. Compute \((1/\sqrt{K})\times[1-\beta\times(Q_0/Q)]\). 10. Compute \(I_{\mathrm{*sm}}\) = (result of step 9) + 1. 11. Output \(I_{\mathrm{*sm}}\).

full_context: When additional capacitor banks with two different reactor ratios are switched on, inrush current calculation should meet the following: let the reactor ratio of the capacitor bank being switched on be K_1. When Q/(K_1 S_d) < 2/3, the inrush current should be calculated as: I_{sm} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{K_1}} + 1.

variable_definitions.name: Peak inrush current
variable_definitions.meaning: Peak inrush current when a capacitor bank is switched on

variable_definitions.name: Reactor ratio of the switched-in bank
variable_definitions.meaning: Reactor ratio of the capacitor bank being switched on

potential_questions: Given K_1=0.05, find I_{sm}
task_description: 1. Determine reactor ratio \(K_1\) of the capacitor bank being switched in. 2. Compute \(1/\sqrt{K_1}\). 3. Compute \(I_{sm}=(1/\sqrt{K_1})+1\). 4. Output \(I_{sm}\).

full_context: The bus voltage rise after a shunt capacitor installation is connected to the grid can be calculated by: ΔU = U_{s0} \frac{Q}{S_d}. Where ΔU is the bus voltage rise (kV); U_{s0} is the bus voltage before capacitor installation is switched on (kV); Q is the total capacity of all capacitor banks operating on the bus (Mvar); S_d is the three-phase short-circuit capacity of the bus (MV·A).

variable_definitions.name: Bus voltage rise
variable_definitions.meaning: Increase in bus voltage caused by switching in capacitors

variable_definitions.name: Bus voltage before switching in
variable_definitions.meaning: Bus voltage before shunt capacitor installation is energized

variable_definitions.name: Total capacitor capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total capacity of all capacitor banks in operation on the bus

variable_definitions.name: Short-circuit capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Three-phase short-circuit capacity of the bus

potential_questions: Given U_{s0}=10kV, Q=20Mvar, S_d=500MV·A, find ΔU
task_description: 1. Obtain bus voltage before switching in \(U_{s0}\). 2. Obtain total capacitor capacity \(Q\). 3. Obtain short-circuit capacity \(S_d\). 4. Compute \(Q/S_d\). 5. Compute \(\Delta U=U_{s0}\times(Q/S_d)\). 6. Output \(\Delta U\).

full_context: The maximum tension of conductor/ground wire at the lowest point of sag should be calculated as: T_{max} = T_p / K_c. Where T_{max} is the maximum tension at the lowest sag point (N); T_p is the breaking force of the conductor/ground wire (N); K_c is the design safety factor for the conductor/ground wire.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum tension
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum tension borne by conductor/ground wire at the lowest sag point

variable_definitions.name: Breaking force
variable_definitions.meaning: Ultimate tensile strength (breaking load) of the conductor/ground wire

variable_definitions.name: Design safety factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Strength margin factor used in engineering design

potential_questions: Given LGJ-400 conductor breaking force 103.4kN and safety factor 2.5, find maximum allowable tension
task_description: 1. Obtain breaking force \(T_p\) for the conductor type. 2. Determine design safety factor \(K_c\). 3. Compute maximum allowable tension \(T_{\max}=T_p/K_c\). 4. Check whether it meets engineering requirements.

full_context: When verification uses breaking stress or breaking bending moment, the stress and bending moment of porcelain bushings and porcelain insulators should satisfy: \sigma_{tot} \leq \sigma_v/1.67. Where \sigma_{tot} is the total stress caused by seismic action and other loads (Pa); \sigma_v is the breaking stress of the equipment or material (Pa).

variable_definitions.name: Total stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Combined stress produced by seismic action and other loads

variable_definitions.name: Breaking stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Ultimate stress at which the material fails

potential_questions: Given porcelain bushing breaking stress 120MPa and combined seismic+wind stress 65MPa, check if it is safe
task_description: 1. Obtain material breaking stress \(\sigma_v\). 2. Determine total combined stress \(\sigma_{\text{tot}}\) from seismic and other loads. 3. Compute allowable stress \(\sigma_v/1.67\). 4. Check whether \(\sigma_{\text{tot}}\le \sigma_v/1.67\). 5. Output verification result.

full_context: The standard value of horizontal wind load on conductors and ground wires should be calculated as: W_x = β_c · W_0 · μ_z · μ_{sc} · μ_c · d · L_p · B · sin²θ. Where W_x is the standard horizontal wind load perpendicular to the conductor direction (kN); β_c is the wind-load adjustment factor for conductor/ground wire; W_0 is the basic wind pressure standard value (kN/m²); μ_z is the wind pressure height-variation factor; μ_{sc} is the shape factor of conductor/ground wire; μ_c is the wind pressure non-uniformity factor; d is the outer diameter of the conductor (m); L_p is the horizontal span of the tower (m); B is the wind-load amplification factor under icing; θ is the angle between wind direction and conductor direction (°).

variable_definitions.name: Horizontal wind load
variable_definitions.meaning: Transverse wind load acting on the conductor

variable_definitions.name: Wind-load adjustment factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor considering wind-speed fluctuation and spatial correlation

variable_definitions.name: Basic wind pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Reference basic wind pressure at standard height

variable_definitions.name: Height-variation factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor reflecting wind speed/pressure variation with height

variable_definitions.name: Shape factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor related to conductor cross-sectional shape

variable_definitions.name: Non-uniformity factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor accounting for non-uniform spatial distribution of wind pressure

variable_definitions.name: Conductor outer diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Calculated diameter of the conductor/ground wire

variable_definitions.name: Horizontal span
variable_definitions.meaning: Average span on both sides of the tower

variable_definitions.name: Icing amplification factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Amplification factor for wind load under icing conditions

variable_definitions.name: Wind-direction angle
variable_definitions.meaning: Angle between wind direction and conductor axis

potential_questions: Calculate wind load for LGJ-400/35 conductor at basic wind speed 30m/s, horizontal span 400m, wind-direction angle 60°
task_description: 1. Determine basic wind pressure \(W_0=V^2/1600\). 2. Obtain coefficients \((\beta_c,\mu_z,\mu_{sc},\mu_c,B)\) from tables. 3. Measure or obtain conductor outer diameter \(d\). 4. Determine horizontal span \(L_p\). 5. Determine wind-direction angle \(\theta\). 6. Compute \(\sin^2\theta\). 7. Substitute into the formula to calculate \(W_x\). 8. Output result.

full_context: Under the maximum calculated sag condition, the minimum distance from conductor to ground should satisfy: D = k_i L_i + U/110 + 0.65\sqrt{f}. Where D is the horizontal phase-to-phase distance (m); k_i is the suspension insulator string factor; L_i is the suspension insulator string length (m); U is the nominal system voltage (kV); f is the maximum sag of the conductor (m).

variable_definitions.name: Horizontal phase-to-phase distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum horizontal spacing between adjacent conductors

variable_definitions.name: Insulator string factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor related to insulator string configuration

variable_definitions.name: Insulator string length
variable_definitions.meaning: Total length of suspension insulator string

variable_definitions.name: System voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Nominal voltage level of transmission line

variable_definitions.name: Conductor sag
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum sag at mid-span

potential_questions: For a 220kV line with insulator string length 2m and sag 15m, calculate minimum horizontal phase spacing
task_description: 1. Determine voltage level \(U\). 2. Measure insulator string length \(L_i\). 3. Determine \(k_i\) (single string typically 0.4). 4. Determine maximum sag \(f\). 5. Compute \(U/110\). 6. Compute \(0.65\sqrt{f}\). 7. Sum \(D=k_i L_i+U/110+0.65\sqrt{f}\). 8. Output result.

full_context: The equivalent horizontal phase spacing for a triangular conductor arrangement should be calculated as: D_x = \sqrt{D_p^2 + \left(\frac{4}{3} D_z\right)^2}. Where D_x is the equivalent horizontal spacing (m); D_p is the horizontal projection spacing (m); D_z is the vertical projection spacing (m).

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent horizontal distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent spacing when triangular arrangement is converted to an equivalent horizontal arrangement

variable_definitions.name: Horizontal projection distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Projected spacing in the horizontal direction

variable_definitions.name: Vertical projection distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Vertical separation between conductors

potential_questions: For a 220kV line with horizontal projection 5m and vertical projection 3m, find equivalent horizontal distance
task_description: 1. Measure horizontal projection distance \(D_p\). 2. Measure vertical projection distance \(D_z\). 3. Compute \((4/3)D_z\). 4. Compute \(D_p^2+[(4/3)D_z]^2\). 5. Take square root to get \(D_x\). 6. Output result.

full_context: The design standard coal consumption rate for power generation of a pure condensing unit should be calculated as: b_{fn} = \frac{0.123}{\eta_{fn}} \times 10^5. Where b_{fn} is the design standard coal consumption rate for power generation of a pure condensing unit (g/(kW·h)); \eta_{fn} is the design power-generation thermal efficiency of the pure condensing unit (%).

variable_definitions.name: Design standard coal consumption rate for power generation
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard coal consumption rate for power generation of a pure condensing unit under design conditions

variable_definitions.name: Design power-generation thermal efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Design power-generation thermal efficiency of a pure condensing unit

potential_questions: Given design power-generation thermal efficiency of a pure condensing unit is 45%, find the design standard coal consumption rate for power generation
task_description: 1. Determine design power-generation thermal efficiency \(\eta_{fn}\). 2. Substitute into formula \(b_{fn}=(0.123/\eta_{fn})\times 10^5\). 3. Calculate \(b_{fn}\). 4. Output result.

full_context: The design power-generation thermal efficiency of a pure condensing unit should be calculated as: \eta_{fn} = \eta_{qn} \eta_{gl} \eta_{gd} \times 10^{-4}. Where \eta_{qn} is boiler efficiency, taken as the guaranteed efficiency specified in the boiler supply contract (%); \eta_{gl} is pipeline efficiency, taken as 99%; \eta_{gd} is turbine efficiency (%).

variable_definitions.name: Design power-generation thermal efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Design power-generation thermal efficiency of a pure condensing unit

variable_definitions.name: Boiler efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Guaranteed boiler efficiency value in the supply contract

variable_definitions.name: Pipeline efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Thermal system pipeline transmission efficiency

variable_definitions.name: Turbine efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Turbine heat-to-work conversion efficiency

potential_questions: Given boiler efficiency 92%, pipeline efficiency 99%, turbine efficiency 45%, find design power-generation thermal efficiency
task_description: 1. Obtain boiler efficiency \(\eta_{qn}\). 2. Set pipeline efficiency \(\eta_{gl}=99\%\). 3. Obtain turbine efficiency \(\eta_{gd}\). 4. Compute \(\eta_{fn}=\eta_{qn}\times\eta_{gl}\times\eta_{gd}\times 10^{-4}\). 5. Output result.

full_context: The thermal efficiency of the turbine-generator set for a pure condensing unit should be calculated as: \eta_{qr} = \frac{3600}{q_{jrr}} \times 100. Where \eta_{qr} is the thermal efficiency of the turbine-generator set of a pure condensing unit (%); q_{jrr} is the design heat rate of the turbine-generator set for a pure condensing unit, taken as the guaranteed heat-rate value corresponding to the acceptance condition specified in the turbine technical agreement (kJ/(kW·h)).

variable_definitions.name: Turbine-generator set thermal efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Thermal efficiency (heat-to-electric conversion) of the turbine-generator set

variable_definitions.name: Design heat rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Design-condition heat consumption rate of the turbine-generator set

potential_questions: Given turbine-generator design heat rate is 8000 kJ/(kW·h), find turbine-generator thermal efficiency
task_description: 1. Obtain design heat rate \(q_{jrr}\). 2. Substitute into formula \(\eta_{qr}=(3600/q_{jrr})\times 100\). 3. Calculate \(\eta_{qr}\). 4. Output result.

full_context: The design standard coal consumption rate for power supply of a pure condensing unit should be calculated as: b_{gn} = \frac{b_{fn}}{1 - \frac{e}{100}}. Where b_{gn} is the design standard coal consumption rate for power supply (g/(kW·h)); e is the auxiliary power ratio of the pure condensing unit (%).

variable_definitions.name: Design standard coal consumption rate for power supply
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard coal consumption rate for net power supply of a pure condensing unit

variable_definitions.name: Design standard coal consumption rate for power generation
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard coal consumption rate for gross power generation of a pure condensing unit

variable_definitions.name: Auxiliary power ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Percentage of plant self-consumption relative to gross generation

keywords: standard coal consumption rate for power supply
potential_questions: Given generation standard coal consumption rate 310 g/(kW·h) and auxiliary power ratio 5%, find supply standard coal consumption rate
task_description: 1. Determine generation standard coal consumption rate \(b_{fn}\). 2. Determine auxiliary power ratio \(e\). 3. Compute \(1-e/100\). 4. Compute \(b_{gn}=b_{fn}/(1-e/100)\). 5. Output result.

full_context: The design standard coal consumption rate for power generation of a CHP (district-heating) unit operating under rated heating conditions should be calculated as: b_{fr} = \frac{0.123}{\eta_{fr}} \times 10^5. Where b_{fr} is the design standard coal consumption rate for power generation under rated heating conditions (g/(kW·h)); \eta_{fr} is the design power-generation thermal efficiency of the CHP unit (%).

variable_definitions.name: Design standard coal consumption rate for power generation
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard coal consumption rate for power generation of a CHP unit under rated heating conditions

variable_definitions.name: Design power-generation thermal efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Design power-generation thermal efficiency of the CHP unit under rated heating conditions

potential_questions: Given CHP unit design power-generation thermal efficiency under rated heating conditions is 50%, find design standard coal consumption rate for power generation
task_description: 1. Determine design power-generation thermal efficiency \(\eta_{fr}\). 2. Substitute into formula \(b_{fr}=(0.123/\eta_{fr})\times 10^5\). 3. Calculate \(b_{fr}\). 4. Output result.

full_context: The design standard coal consumption rate for heat supply of a CHP unit should be calculated as: b_r = \frac{34.16}{\eta_{gl} \eta_{gc} \eta_{hs}} \times 10^6. Where b_r is the design standard coal consumption rate for heat supply (kg/GJ); \eta_{gl} is boiler efficiency (%); \eta_{gc} is pipeline efficiency (%); \eta_{hs} is the heat-exchange efficiency of the primary heat-exchange station of the district heating network (%).

variable_definitions.name: Design standard coal consumption rate for heat supply
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard coal consumption rate for heat supply of a CHP unit

variable_definitions.name: Boiler efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Boiler thermal efficiency

variable_definitions.name: Pipeline efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: District heating pipeline transmission efficiency

variable_definitions.name: Primary station heat-exchange efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat exchanger efficiency at the primary station of the heating network

keywords: standard coal consumption rate for heat supply
potential_questions: Given boiler efficiency 92%, pipeline efficiency 98%, primary station heat-exchange efficiency 95%, find standard coal consumption rate for heat supply
task_description: 1. Determine boiler efficiency \(\eta_{gl}\). 2. Determine pipeline efficiency \(\eta_{gc}\). 3. Determine primary station heat-exchange efficiency \(\eta_{hs}\). 4. Compute \(b_r=\frac{34.16}{\eta_{gl}\times\eta_{gc}\times\eta_{hs}}\times 10^6\). 5. Output result.

full_context: In a PV array, electrical performance parameters of modules in the same string should preferably be consistent. The number of modules connected in series in a PV string should be calculated as: N ≤ V_{dcmax}/(V_{oc}×[1+(t-25)×K_v]) (Eq. 6.4.2-1). Where N is the number of series-connected PV modules (integer); V_{dcmax} is the inverter maximum DC input voltage; V_{oc} is the module open-circuit voltage; t is the module operating extreme low temperature (°C); K_v is the open-circuit voltage temperature coefficient.

variable_definitions.name: Number of modules in series
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of PV modules connected in series in a string

variable_definitions.name: Inverter maximum DC input voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum allowable DC input voltage limit of the inverter

variable_definitions.name: Module open-circuit voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Output voltage of PV module at open circuit

variable_definitions.name: Operating extreme low temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum temperature limit for PV module operation environment

variable_definitions.name: Open-circuit voltage temperature coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient describing variation of V_oc with temperature

potential_questions: Given V_{dcmax}=1000V, V_{oc}=45V, t=-10°C, K_v=-0.003, find maximum series number N
task_description: 1. Determine inverter parameter \(V_{dcmax}\). 2. Obtain module open-circuit voltage \(V_{oc}\). 3. Determine operating extreme low temperature \(t\). 4. Obtain temperature coefficient \(K_v\). 5. Compute temperature correction factor \([1+(t-25)\times K_v]\). 6. Compute denominator \(V_{oc}\times[1+(t-25)\times K_v]\). 7. Compute \(N_{\max}=V_{dcmax}/\text{(denominator)}\). 8. Round \(N_{\max}\) down to an integer.

full_context: The maximum power point operating voltage range of a PV string should satisfy: V_{mpptmin}/(V_{pm}×[1+(t'-25)×K_v']) ≤ N ≤ V_{mpptmax}/(V_{pm}×[1+(t-25)×K_v']) (Eq. 6.4.2-2). Where V_{mpptmin} and V_{mpptmax} are the inverter MPPT voltage range; V_{pm} is the module operating voltage; t' is the operating extreme high temperature (°C); K_v' is the operating-voltage temperature coefficient; t is the operating extreme low temperature (°C) (same as defined above).

variable_definitions.name: Minimum MPPT voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum operating voltage for inverter maximum power point tracking

variable_definitions.name: Maximum MPPT voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum operating voltage for inverter maximum power point tracking

variable_definitions.name: Module operating voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: PV module voltage at rated operating point

variable_definitions.name: Operating extreme high temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum temperature limit for PV module operation environment

variable_definitions.name: Operating voltage temperature coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient describing variation of operating voltage with temperature

variable_definitions.name: Operating extreme low temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined above: minimum temperature limit for PV module operation environment

potential_questions: Given V_{mpptmin}=500V, V_{mpptmax}=800V, V_{pm}=35V, t'=60°C, t=-10°C, K_v'=-0.004, find allowable range of N
task_description: 1. Determine inverter MPPT parameters \(V_{mpptmin}\) and \(V_{mpptmax}\). 2. Obtain module operating voltage \(V_{pm}\). 3. Determine temperature extremes \(t\) and \(t'\). 4. Obtain temperature coefficient \(K_v'\). 5. Compute high-temperature correction factor \([1+(t'-25)\times K_v']\). 6. Compute low-temperature correction factor \([1+(t-25)\times K_v']\). 7. Compute \(N_{\min}=V_{mpptmin}/(V_{pm}\times\text{high-temp correction})\). 8. Compute \(N_{\max}=V_{mpptmax}/(V_{pm}\times\text{low-temp correction})\). 9. Report allowable integer range for \(N\) based on \([N_{\min},N_{\max}]\).

full_context: Energy storage battery capacity calculation for a stand-alone PV power station: C_s = (D × F × P_d)/(U × K_a). Where C_s is battery capacity (kW·h); D is the maximum number of consecutive hours without sunshine requiring power supply (h); F is discharge efficiency correction factor (take 1.05); P_d is average load power (kW); U is depth of discharge (0.5–0.8); K_a is AC circuit loss factor (0.7–0.8).

variable_definitions.name: Battery capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total energy storage capability of the battery system

variable_definitions.name: No-sunshine duration
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum continuous time the system must supply power without solar input

variable_definitions.name: Efficiency correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor correcting for battery discharge efficiency losses

variable_definitions.name: Average load power
variable_definitions.meaning: Average power demand of the load

variable_definitions.name: Depth of discharge
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum allowable fraction of battery discharge

variable_definitions.name: AC loss factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Efficiency loss factor of inverter and other AC circuits

potential_questions: A stand-alone system requires D=72h, P_d=5kW, choose U=0.7, K_a=0.75, F=1.05; find battery capacity C_s
task_description: 1. Determine no-sunshine duration \(D\). 2. Determine average load power \(P_d\). 3. Select depth of discharge \(U\) (0.5–0.8). 4. Determine AC loss factor \(K_a\) (0.7–0.8). 5. Use efficiency correction factor \(F=1.05\). 6. Compute numerator \(D\times F\times P_d\). 7. Compute denominator \(U\times K_a\). 8. Compute \(C_s=(D\times F\times P_d)/(U\times K_a)\).

full_context: Annual grid-delivered energy calculation for a PV power station: E_p = H_A × (P_{AZ}/E_s) × K. Where E_p is grid-delivered energy (kW·h); H_A is total annual horizontal-plane irradiation (kW·h/m²); P_{AZ} is installed PV capacity (kW_p); E_s is standard irradiance (1kW/m²); K is overall efficiency factor.

variable_definitions.name: Grid-delivered energy
variable_definitions.meaning: Annual electrical energy delivered from PV plant to the grid

variable_definitions.name: Total horizontal-plane irradiation
variable_definitions.meaning: Total annual solar irradiation received per unit area on horizontal plane

variable_definitions.name: Installed capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Sum of nominal power ratings of PV modules

variable_definitions.name: Standard irradiance
variable_definitions.meaning: Reference irradiance under STC

variable_definitions.name: Overall efficiency factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Derating factor including all system losses

potential_questions: For a PV plant with H_A=1600kW·h/m², P_{AZ}=100MW_p, K=0.75, find annual grid-delivered energy E_p
task_description: 1. Obtain local annual total horizontal irradiation \(H_A\). 2. Determine total installed PV capacity \(P_{AZ}\). 3. Determine overall efficiency factor \(K\) (including degradation, soiling, etc.). 4. Compute equivalent peak-sun-hours \(H_A/E_s\). 5. Compute theoretical energy \((H_A/E_s)\times P_{AZ}\). 6. Compute \(E_p=\text{theoretical energy}\times K\).

full_context: Limit state check for load-bearing capacity of PV mounting structure: γ_0 × S ≤ R, where γ_0 is the importance factor (≥0.95), S is the design value of combined load effects, and R is the design value of member resistance.

variable_definitions.name: Importance factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor reflecting structural safety class

variable_definitions.name: Combined load effect
variable_definitions.meaning: Structural response under load combination

variable_definitions.name: Member resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum load effect the structural member can resist

potential_questions: Given γ_0=0.95, S=120kN·m, R=150kN·m, check whether γ_0S≤R
task_description: 1. Determine structural importance factor \(\gamma_0\). 2. Determine combined load effect design value \(S\). 3. Determine member resistance design value \(R\). 4. Compute \(\gamma_0\times S\). 5. Compare \(\gamma_0 S\) with \(R\). 6. If \(\gamma_0 S \le R\), the requirement is satisfied.

formula: S = γ_G S_{GK} + γ_w ψ_w S_{wK} + γ_s ψ_s S_{sK} + γ_t ψ_t S_{tK}
full_context: Load combination without seismic action: S = γ_G × S_{GK} + γ_w × ψ_w × S_{wK} + γ_s × ψ_s × S_{sK} + γ_t × ψ_t × S_{tK}. Here γ_G is the partial factor for permanent load; S_{GK} is the characteristic value of permanent load; γ_w, γ_s, γ_t = 1.4 are partial factors for wind/snow/temperature loads; ψ is the combination factor; S_{wK}, etc. are the characteristic values of the corresponding loads.

variable_definitions.name: Combined load effect
variable_definitions.meaning: Structural response under load combination

variable_definitions.name: Permanent load partial factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Amplification factor for permanent load

variable_definitions.name: Permanent load characteristic value
variable_definitions.meaning: Representative (characteristic) value of permanent load

variable_definitions.name: Variable load partial factors
variable_definitions.meaning: Amplification factors for wind/snow/temperature loads

variable_definitions.name: Combination factors
variable_definitions.meaning: Reduction factors for variable loads in combination

variable_definitions.name: Load characteristic values
variable_definitions.meaning: Representative values of wind/snow/temperature loads

potential_questions: Given γ_G=1.2, S_{GK}=80kN, γ_w=1.4, ψ_w=1.0, S_{wK}=30kN, find S (ignore other loads)
task_description: 1. Determine partial factors \(\gamma\) for each load. 2. Obtain characteristic values \(S_K\) for each load. 3. Determine combination factors \(\psi\). 4. Compute each term \(\gamma\times\psi\times S_K\). 5. Sum all terms to obtain \(S\).

full_context: Theoretical annual energy production of a wind farm: E_{th} = 8760 × ∑_{i=1}^{n} ∫_{v_1}^{v_2} p_i(v) × f_i(v) dv. Where E_{th} is theoretical energy production (MW·h); n is number of turbines; v_1/v_2 are cut-in/cut-out wind speeds (m/s); p_i(v) is the power curve of turbine i (MW); f_i(v) is the wind speed probability distribution function.

variable_definitions.name: Theoretical annual energy production
variable_definitions.meaning: Annual theoretical energy production without considering losses

variable_definitions.name: Number of turbines
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of wind turbine generators in the wind farm

variable_definitions.name: Cut-in wind speed
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum wind speed at which the turbine starts generating power

variable_definitions.name: Cut-out wind speed
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum wind speed at which the turbine stops generating power

variable_definitions.name: Turbine power curve
variable_definitions.meaning: Function of turbine output power versus wind speed

variable_definitions.name: Wind speed probability distribution
variable_definitions.meaning: Probability density function of wind speed at hub height

potential_questions: A wind farm has 30 turbines, cut-in speed 3m/s, cut-out speed 25m/s. Single-turbine power curve p(v)=0.02(v-3)² (3≤v<15) and p(v)=3.6 (15≤v≤25). Wind speed follows Weibull distribution with shape k=2 and scale c=8m/s. Find E_{th}.
task_description: 1. Determine number of turbines \(n\). 2. Set cut-in wind speed \(v_1\) and cut-out wind speed \(v_2\). 3. Obtain power curve function \(p(v)\). 4. Determine wind speed probability density function \(f(v)\). 5. For each turbine, compute \(\int_{v_1}^{v_2} p(v) f(v)\,dv\). 6. Sum results for \(i=1\) to \(n\). 7. Multiply by 8760 hours to obtain \(E_{th}\).

full_context: Conversion formula for maximum wind speed at standard air density: V_{std} = V_{mea} × √(ρ_m/ρ_0), where V_{std} is wind speed at standard density (m/s), V_{mea} is the measured maximum wind speed (m/s), ρ_m is the site air density (kg/m³), and ρ_0=1.225kg/m³ is the standard air density.

variable_definitions.name: Wind speed at standard density
variable_definitions.meaning: Wind speed converted to standard air density

variable_definitions.name: Measured maximum wind speed
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum wind speed observed on site

variable_definitions.name: Site air density
variable_definitions.meaning: Air density during the observation period

variable_definitions.name: Standard air density
variable_definitions.meaning: Reference standard air density value

potential_questions: Measured V_{mea}=40m/s, ρ_m=1.18kg/m³, find V_{std}
task_description: 1. Obtain measured maximum wind speed \(V_{mea}\). 2. Measure site air density \(\rho_m\). 3. Use standard air density \(\rho_0=1.225\,\mathrm{kg/m^3}\). 4. Compute density ratio \(\rho_m/\rho_0\). 5. Take square root \(\sqrt{\rho_m/\rho_0}\). 6. Compute \(V_{std}=V_{mea}\times\sqrt{\rho_m/\rho_0}\).

full_context: Formula for the 50-year return period maximum wind speed at 10m above ground: v_0 = √(2000 × ω_0 / ρ), where v_0 is the maximum wind speed at 10m height (m/s), ω_0 is the 50-year return period basic wind pressure (kN/m²), and ρ is the annual average air density (kg/m³).

variable_definitions.name: Wind speed at 10m height
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum wind speed at 10m above ground

variable_definitions.name: Basic wind pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: 50-year return period basic wind pressure value

variable_definitions.name: Annual average air density
variable_definitions.meaning: Annual average air density observed by the meteorological station

potential_questions: Given ω_0=0.45kN/m², ρ=1.2kg/m³, find v_0
task_description: 1. Obtain the 50-year return period basic wind pressure \(\omega_0\). 2. Determine annual average air density \(\rho\). 3. Compute \(2000\times\omega_0\). 4. Compute \((2000\times\omega_0)/\rho\). 5. Take square root to get \(v_0=\sqrt{2000\times\omega_0/\rho}\).

full_context: For steel structure design by the ultimate limit state of load-bearing capacity, the basic combination of load effects should be considered, with design expression: γ_0 S ≤ R. Here γ_0 is the structural importance factor (Safety Class I ≥1.1, Class II ≥1.0, Class III ≥0.9); S is the design value of the combined load effects; R is the design resistance of the structural member. This formula is used for ultimate limit state verification to ensure the structure does not fail in strength or stability under load effects.

variable_definitions.name: Structural importance factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor reflecting the structural safety class

variable_definitions.name: Combined load effect
variable_definitions.meaning: Structural response value under combined loads

variable_definitions.name: Member resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum load effect the structural member can resist

keywords: ultimate limit state of load-bearing capacity
potential_questions: Given γ_0=1.1, S=120kN·m, R=150kN·m, check whether γ_0S≤R
task_description: 1. Determine structural importance factor \(\gamma_0\). 2. Calculate design value of combined load effect \(S\). 3. Determine design resistance \(R\) of the member. 4. Compute \(\gamma_0\times S\). 5. Compare \(\gamma_0 S\) with \(R\). 6. If \(\gamma_0 S \le R\), the requirement is satisfied.

full_context: For seismic verification, the horizontal seismic story shear of any floor of a structure should satisfy: V_{EK,i} \geq \lambda \sum_{j=i}^{n} G_j. Where V_{EK,i} is the standard value of horizontal seismic story shear for story i; \lambda is the minimum story shear coefficient (value taken from Table 5.2.5); G_j is the representative value of gravity load at story j. The formula ensures adequate seismic capacity at each story to prevent weak-story failure.

variable_definitions.name: Seismic story shear at story i
variable_definitions.meaning: Story shear at story i corresponding to the standard value of horizontal seismic action

variable_definitions.name: Minimum seismic story shear coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Lower-bound ratio of story shear to gravity load

variable_definitions.name: Representative gravity load at story j
variable_definitions.meaning: Representative value of gravity load at story j

potential_questions: For a frame in seismic intensity 6 region, with ΣG_j=8000kN from story i to n and \lambda=0.008, find minimum V_{EK,i}
task_description: 1. Identify story \(i\). 2. Compute \(\sum_{j=i}^{n} G_j\). 3. Obtain \(\lambda\) from the table. 4. Compute minimum shear \(V_{\min}=\lambda\times\sum_{j=i}^{n} G_j\). 5. Compare design shear \(V_{EK,i}\) with \(V_{\min}\).

full_context: In the flexural bearing capacity calculation of beam normal sections, for frame beams with Seismic Grade I, the height of the concrete compression zone should satisfy: x \leq 0.25h_0. Where x is the height of the concrete compression zone; h_0 is the effective section depth. This requirement ensures sufficient ductility in the plastic hinge region at beam ends, preventing concrete crushing before steel yielding.

variable_definitions.name: Concrete compression zone height
variable_definitions.meaning: Calculated height of the compression zone in the beam section

variable_definitions.name: Effective section depth
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance from resultant tensile reinforcement to the compression edge

potential_questions: For a Seismic Grade I beam with h_0=600mm, find maximum allowable x
task_description: 1. Determine seismic grade. 2. Measure effective section depth \(h_0\). 3. Compute \(x_{\max}=0.25h_0\). 4. Control design so that actual \(x\le x_{\max}\).

full_context: In stress analysis of concrete arch dams, the maximum principal compressive stress of the dam body should satisfy: \sigma_{max} \leq f_{ck}/\gamma_m. Where f_{ck} is the characteristic value of axial compressive strength of concrete; \gamma_m is the partial factor for material properties (take 1.5). This formula limits compressive stress to the allowable material value to ensure compressive safety.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum principal compressive stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum compressive stress at the calculated dam section

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic compressive strength of concrete
variable_definitions.meaning: Characteristic value of axial compressive strength of concrete

variable_definitions.name: Material partial factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety factor accounting for variability of material properties

potential_questions: For a C25 concrete arch dam (f_{ck}=16.7MPa), find allowable \sigma_{max}
task_description: 1. Determine concrete strength grade. 2. Obtain \(f_{ck}\) from tables. 3. Use \(\gamma_m=1.5\). 4. Compute allowable stress \([f]=f_{ck}/\gamma_m\). 5. Control design stress \(\sigma_{\max}\le [f]\).

full_context: Design value of action effects under the basic combination: S_d = \gamma_G S_{Gk} + \gamma_Q S_{Qk}. Where \gamma_G is the partial factor for permanent load (take 1.2 for unfavorable, 1.0 for favorable); \gamma_Q is the partial factor for variable load (generally 1.4); S_{Gk} and S_{Qk} are the characteristic values of effects from permanent and variable loads. The formula is used for action effect combination in ultimate limit state design of hydraulic structures.

variable_definitions.name: Design value of action effect
variable_definitions.meaning: Design value of structural response under load combination

variable_definitions.name: Permanent load partial factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Amplification factor for permanent load

variable_definitions.name: Variable load partial factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Amplification factor for variable load

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic effect of permanent load
variable_definitions.meaning: Characteristic value of structural response caused by permanent load

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic effect of variable load
variable_definitions.meaning: Characteristic value of structural response caused by variable load

potential_questions: Given S_{Gk}=80kN·m, S_{Qk}=30kN·m, \gamma_G=1.2, \gamma_Q=1.4, find S_d
task_description: 1. Determine permanent load effect \(S_{Gk}\). 2. Determine variable load effect \(S_{Qk}\). 3. Select \(\gamma_G\) and \(\gamma_Q\). 4. Compute \(S_d=\gamma_G S_{Gk}+\gamma_Q S_{Qk}\).

full_context: When checking formwork stiffness, the maximum deformation shall not exceed the following allowable values: (1) for formwork with exposed concrete surfaces, 1/400 of the calculated span of the formwork member; (2) for formwork with concealed concrete surfaces, 1/250 of the calculated span of the formwork member. This rule controls formwork deformation during concrete pouring within allowable limits.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum formwork deformation
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum deflection of formwork under load

variable_definitions.name: Calculated formwork span
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance between formwork support points

potential_questions: Given L=4000mm, find allowable maximum deformation for exposed formwork
task_description: 1. Determine formwork type (exposed/concealed). 2. Measure calculated span \(L\). 3. Select allowable deformation factor (exposed \(1/400\), concealed \(1/250\)). 4. Compute \(f_{\max}=L/400\) or \(f_{\max}=L/250\). 5. Output allowable deformation \(f_{\max}\).

formula: F(X) = \bar{X}(1 - t C_v) \geq R \quad \text{or} \quad F(X) = \bar{X} - t S \geq R \quad \text{or} \quad X_{\text{min}} \geq K R
full_context: Roller-compacted concrete quality assessment should be based on compressive strength at the design age. For a large sample (continuous sampling >30 groups), the evaluation function is: F(X)=X̄(1-tC_v)≥R or F(X)=X̄-tS≥R or X_min≥KR. This formula is used for quality assessment of RCC compressive strength to ensure strength meets design requirements.

variable_definitions.name: Average compressive strength
variable_definitions.meaning: Arithmetic mean of compressive strength over N tests

variable_definitions.name: Strength assurance factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor related to number of tests N and assurance level P(X)

variable_definitions.name: Coefficient of variation
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of standard deviation to mean

variable_definitions.name: Standard deviation
variable_definitions.meaning: Standard deviation of compressive strength

variable_definitions.name: Concrete strength grade
variable_definitions.meaning: Strength value corresponding to the design grade

variable_definitions.name: Minimum compressive strength
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum compressive strength among N tests

variable_definitions.name: Safety factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor related to strength assurance level

potential_questions: Given X̄=25MPa, C_v=0.15, t=1.645, R=20MPa, determine whether F(X)≥R
task_description: 1. Compute average compressive strength \(\bar{X}\). 2. Determine strength assurance factor \(t\) (from table). 3. Compute coefficient of variation \(C_v\) or standard deviation \(S\). 4. Compute \(F(X)\) using the selected formula. 5. Compare \(F(X)\) with \(R\), or compare \(X_{\min}\) with \(K R\). 6. Determine whether the requirement \(\ge R\) is satisfied.

formula: \gamma_d = \frac{\text{Characteristic value of material strength}}{\text{Design value of material strength}}
full_context: For design by ultimate limit state of load-bearing capacity, the structural coefficient γ_d should be determined from Table 8.0.5 based on pipe type, stress type, and internal force type. The formula is used for safety factor calculation of pressure steel pipes to ensure bearing safety under various conditions.

variable_definitions.name: Structural coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient reflecting safety margin of material strength

potential_questions: Given characteristic material strength =450MPa and design strength =300MPa, find γ_d
task_description: 1. Obtain characteristic value of material strength. 2. Obtain design value of material strength. 3. Compute \(\gamma_d=\text{characteristic}/\text{design}\). 4. Output structural coefficient \(\gamma_d\).

full_context: Slope stability analysis for hydropower and water conservancy projects should use the lower-bound solution of the limit equilibrium method, and the design safety factor K should not be less than the value specified in Table 5.0.4. The formula is used for calculating slope anti-sliding stability safety factor to ensure stability under various load combinations.

variable_definitions.name: Anti-sliding stability safety factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of sliding resistance to driving (sliding) force

variable_definitions.name: Allowable safety factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum safety factor specified by the code

potential_questions: Given sliding resistance=1200kN, driving force=800kN, [K]=1.25, verify whether K≥[K]
task_description: 1. Compute total sliding resistance. 2. Compute total driving (sliding) force. 3. Compute \(K=\text{sliding resistance}/\text{driving force}\). 4. Obtain \([K]\) from the table. 5. Compare \(K\) with \([K]\).

full_context: In areas with altitude not exceeding 1000m, the minimum clearance d between live parts and tower members should satisfy Table 7.0.9. This formula is used to calculate the clearance correction for high-altitude regions to ensure insulation coordination meets safety requirements.

variable_definitions.name: Minimum electrical clearance
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum distance between live parts and grounded components

variable_definitions.name: Nominal system voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated voltage of the power system

potential_questions: Given U_N=220kV, find minimum d
task_description: 1. Determine nominal system voltage \(U_N\). 2. Compute base clearance \(d_0=U_N/150+8\). 3. Determine correction factor based on altitude. 4. Compute \(d=d_0\times\text{correction factor}\). 5. Output minimum electrical clearance \(d\).

full_context: Conductor cross-section should generally be selected based on economic current density, and additionally the line corona characteristic parameters should be checked. Corona characteristic parameters include environmental impact parameters such as corona loss, radio interference, television interference, electric field effects, and audible noise. Calculation of line corona loss is given in Appendix B. The modified Peek formula (for bipolar lines under fair weather) is shown as Eq. (B.1): P = \frac{K}{A} \sqrt{\frac{r^*}{s}} \left[ U - (g_0 m_0 r^*) \ln\left(\frac{s}{r^*}\right) \right]^2 \times 10^{-6}. This formula is used to calculate corona loss of HVDC lines under fair-weather conditions, applicable to regions with altitude not exceeding 1000m.

variable_definitions.name: Line corona loss
variable_definitions.meaning: Corona power loss per unit line length

variable_definitions.name: Empirical coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Constant determined by experiments

variable_definitions.name: Atmospheric correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction for the influence of atmospheric conditions on corona

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent conductor radius
variable_definitions.meaning: Radius equivalent to the corona inception voltage of bundled conductors

variable_definitions.name: Pole spacing
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance between positive and negative pole conductors

variable_definitions.name: Conductor-to-ground operating voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Operating voltage of the conductor relative to ground

variable_definitions.name: Corona inception gradient
variable_definitions.meaning: Critical potential gradient for corona inception at conductor surface

variable_definitions.name: Surface roughness factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor reflecting conductor surface roughness

potential_questions: For a ±500kV DC line: s=1400cm, r*=2.5cm, U=500kV, pressure p=100kPa, temperature θ=25°C, calculate corona loss P
task_description: 1. Compute atmospheric correction factor \(A=2.94\times 100/(273+25)\). 2. Compute corona term \(g_0 m_0 r^*=29.8\times 0.47\times 2.5\). 3. Compute \(\ln(s/r^*)=\ln(1400/2.5)\). 4. Compute bracket term \([U-(g_0 m_0 r^*)\times\ln(s/r^*)]\). 5. Compute \(\sqrt{r^*/s}=\sqrt{2.5/1400}\). 6. Compute \(P=(K/A)\times\sqrt{r^*/s}\times[\cdots]^2\times 10^{-6}\) (with \(K=206\)). 7. Output \(P\).

full_context: CIGRE recommended method: first compute the equivalent diameter of bundled conductors d_eq = D (nd/D)^{1/2}, then obtain the equivalent total charge Q using the Maxwell potential coefficient method, and compute average surface potential gradient g = Q/(π ε₀ d n). The maximum surface potential gradient is determined by Eq. (C.4): g_{max} = g [1 + (n-1)(d/D)]. This formula is used to determine the maximum surface potential gradient of HVDC conductors, a key parameter for evaluating corona characteristics.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum surface potential gradient
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum electric field strength on the conductor surface

variable_definitions.name: Average surface potential gradient
variable_definitions.meaning: Average electric field strength on the conductor surface

variable_definitions.name: Number of sub-conductors
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of sub-conductors in the bundle

variable_definitions.name: Sub-conductor diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Diameter of a single sub-conductor

variable_definitions.name: Bundle circle diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Diameter of the circle formed by bundled sub-conductors

variable_definitions.name: Equivalent total charge
variable_definitions.meaning: Total charge obtained by the potential coefficient method

variable_definitions.name: Vacuum permittivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Fundamental physical constant of dielectric media

potential_questions: For a 4-bundle conductor with D=45cm, d=3cm, g=15kV/cm, find g_{max}
task_description: 1. Determine bundle number \(n=4\). 2. Obtain average gradient \(g=15\,\mathrm{kV/cm}\). 3. Compute \(d/D=3/45\). 4. Compute \((n-1)\times(d/D)=3\times(3/45)\). 5. Compute \(g_{\max}=15\times[1+(\text{result of step 4})]\). 6. Output \(g_{\max}\).

full_context: Generator voltage regulation (static voltage droop rate, i.e., voltage regulation accuracy under load change) refers to the rate of change of generator terminal voltage when, with the droop unit of the AVR removed, voltage reference unchanged, and at rated power factor, the load decreases from rated apparent power to zero. It is defined by: ε(%) = [(U_{G0}-U_{GN})/U_{GN}]×100%, where U_{G0} is no-load terminal voltage and U_{GN} is terminal voltage at rated apparent power. This parameter is an important indicator for evaluating voltage regulation accuracy of the excitation system.

variable_definitions.name: Static voltage droop rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage change rate caused by load variation

variable_definitions.name: No-load terminal voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Generator terminal voltage when apparent power is zero

variable_definitions.name: Rated terminal voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Generator terminal voltage at rated apparent power

potential_questions: Generator rated terminal voltage U_{GN}=20kV, no-load voltage U_{G0}=21kV, find static droop rate
task_description: 1. Measure no-load voltage \(U_{G0}=21\,\mathrm{kV}\). 2. Record rated voltage \(U_{GN}=20\,\mathrm{kV}\). 3. Compute voltage difference \(\Delta U=21-20\). 4. Compute \(\varepsilon=(\Delta U/20)\times 100\%\). 5. Output \(\varepsilon\).

full_context: Formula for radio interference field strength of a single-phase conductor: E = 3.5g_{max} + 12r - 30. Where E is the radio interference field strength at a point 20m from the conductor and 2m above ground (dB(μV/m)); g_{max} is the RMS value of maximum surface potential gradient of a sub-conductor (kV/cm); r is the sub-conductor radius (cm). This formula applies to the 12–20 kV/cm gradient range, and the result represents the fair-weather average interference at 0.5MHz.

variable_definitions.name: Radio interference field strength
variable_definitions.meaning: Interference intensity at 20m from the conductor near ground

variable_definitions.name: Maximum surface potential gradient
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum electric field strength on the conductor surface

variable_definitions.name: Sub-conductor radius
variable_definitions.meaning: Radius of a single sub-conductor

potential_questions: Given g_{max}=15kV/cm and r=1.5cm, find interference field strength E at 20m
task_description: 1. Obtain \(g_{\max}=15\,\mathrm{kV/cm}\). 2. Obtain \(r=1.5\,\mathrm{cm}\). 3. Compute \(3.5\times g_{\max}\). 4. Compute \(12\times r\). 5. Compute \(E=\text{(step 3)}+\text{(step 4)}-30\). 6. Output \(E\).
